summary: Hernando, Mississippi, was where my aunt and uncle lived, just a little south of Nesbit. My uncle worked on a dairy farm there but eventually had to leave because of threats after a verbal altercation with the owner. It was a small place tied to sharecropping, and my family had roots there, with my uncle carving much of the furniture in their wooden house. Despite the challenges, it was part of my early life and family history.

I grew up near Nesbit, Mississippi, and it was about twenty miles north of Hernando where my family lived. I remember being in Nesbit when I heard about Billie Jean, the country singer, making a karaoke track, and it struck me how our cultures—hers in Nashville or the Ozarks and mine in Nesbit—were connected through music. Nesbit was part of my childhood landscape, and I recall many experiences there, including going to the local drugstore and the realities of segregation.

My first regular school was in Memphis, Tennessee, where I attended kindergarten in South Memphis with Miss Potts. Later, my uncle moved to Memphis after leaving Hernando due to threats at his job. Memphis was a significant place in my early education and family life, bridging my experiences between small-town Mississippi and a larger city environment.

I discovered a karaoke track made by Billie Jean, the country singer, who said she got it when she was a young girl singing that song. It amazed me how her music from places like Nashville or the Ozarks connected with my life in Nesbit, Mississippi. Billie Jean’s music was part of the cultural tapestry I grew up with, even though our backgrounds were quite different.

Nashville, Tennessee, came up in my story when I thought about the music cultures blending, especially with Billie Jean, the country singer, who was from Nashville or the Ozarks. It was fascinating to me how someone from Nashville could be connected through music to my life in Nesbit, Mississippi. Nashville represented a place of musical influence that I admired from afar.

The Ozarks were mentioned as part of the region where Billie Jean, the country singer, might have been when she recorded her karaoke track. It struck me how the Ozarks and places like Nashville were linked to my own experiences in Nesbit, Mississippi, through music. The Ozarks symbolized another cultural area that influenced the music I loved and connected me to a broader American tradition.

As a young child, I loved listening to The Firestone Hour on the radio, especially during certain hours when the grounding wire would pick up the station. Marguerite Piazza would open the show with a beautiful song, and I was fascinated by the music even though it was different from the gospel and quartet music of my African American heritage. The Firestone Hour was a special part of my childhood that introduced me to a wider range of music.

Marguerite Piazza was the woman who opened The Firestone Hour with a lovely song that I loved as a child. Her voice and the music she sang fascinated me because it was different from the gospel music I grew up with in my African American community. Hearing her sing made me look forward to the radio show and inspired my early love of music.

Growing up African American in Mississippi, I was raised with gospel and quartet music that reflected my heritage. My family and community taught me the importance of character and citizenship, supporting my love of music and education. Despite the segregation and restrictions we faced, the African American culture was a strong foundation for me, shaping my values and identity throughout my life.

As a teenager in the 1960s, I witnessed the beginnings of the Civil Rights Movement and was happy to be part of the positive change happening in our country. I remember the challenges we faced with segregation—like separate drinking fountains and bus seating—but also the hope that came with people standing up for what was right. The movement taught me that good people must unite and resist injustice peacefully, and that has stayed with me as a guiding principle.
